title: Dark Matter and Dark Energy
subtitle: The unseen majority of the universe

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: How do scientists know dark matter exists if it cannot be seen?
options: Dark matter emits a faint red glow detected by infrared telescopes
It is inferred from its gravitational effects on visible matter and light
Dark matter was directly detected in particle accelerator experiments
Scientists do not actually believe dark matter exists; it is just a theory
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Dark matter does not emit, absorb, or reflect light — it is truly invisible. Its existence is inferred from gravitational effects: galaxies rotate faster than visible mass alone can explain, and gravitational lensing of light is stronger than expected.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Dark matter is inferred from its gravitational effects — for example, galaxies spin too fast to be held together by visible matter alone.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.8.3 — Dark matter and dark energy (Higher)

steps.title: Dark Matter and Dark Energy
steps.bodyHTML: Dark matter is an unknown form of mass that does not interact with light; its existence is inferred from the gravitational effects it has on the rotation of galaxies and the bending (gravitational lensing) of light passing massive objects. Dark energy is an unknown form of energy thought to be responsible for the accelerating expansion of the universe — observations show distant supernovae are further away than expected, suggesting expansion is speeding up. Together, dark matter and dark energy account for approximately 95% of the total mass-energy content of the universe.
commonErrors: Thinking dark matter is the same as dark energy — they are completely different phenomena
Thinking dark matter has been directly observed — it is only inferred from its effects
Forgetting that together dark matter and dark energy make up about 95% of the universe

aqa.questionText: Scientists observe that the outer stars of a galaxy rotate much faster than can be explained by the mass of visible stars. Describe what this suggests and how it provides evidence for dark matter.

steps.label: Identify the observation
steps.content: The outer stars orbit much faster than expected based on the gravitational effect of all visible mass in the galaxy.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — outer stars rotate faster than predicted by visible mass

steps.label: Explain the implication
steps.content: There must be additional, unseen mass providing extra gravitational force to keep those stars in orbit at high speed.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — additional unseen mass must be present

steps.label: Link to dark matter
steps.content: This unseen mass is called dark matter. It does not emit or absorb light, so it cannot be detected directly, only inferred from its gravitational effects.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — inferred mass is dark matter (undetectable by light)

aqa.finalAnswer: Outer stars orbit too fast for visible mass alone → additional unseen (dark) matter provides extra gravitational force.

aqa.questionText: What is the role of dark energy in modern cosmology?

options.label: A
options.text: It provides the gravitational force that holds galaxies together

options.label: B
options.text: It is the energy released during the Big Bang that still heats the universe

options.label: C
options.text: It is thought to be responsible for the accelerating expansion of the universe

options.label: D
options.text: It is the energy stored inside dark matter particles

markSchemeSteps: Dark energy is the name given to whatever is causing the universe’s expansion to accelerate ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option A describes dark matter (gravitational effects), not dark energy. The two are distinct.
